original_abstract: The cultural and anthropological essence of Turkic Sufism is built upon the quest for spiritual perfection, the pursuit of the absolute, and the archetype of the "perfect human" (*insan-i kamil*). While historically significant, the sociological mechanisms behind its integration into nomadic life remain under-researched. This article re-evaluates Sufi motives within ancient Turkic nomadic culture by applying the sociological lenses of collective ritual, charismatic authority, and cultural capital. Drawing on Émile Durkheim’s theory of collective effervescence, we analyze how communal Sufi practices—such as the *zikr* circle and sacred dance—transformed abstract beliefs into shared emotional experiences that fortified social cohesion. Utilizing Max Weber’s concept of charismatic authority, we examine the pivotal role of Sufi saints whose perceived spiritual extraordinary qualities allowed them to bridge tribal divides and legitimize a new religious order. Furthermore, through Pierre Bourdieu’s framework of cultural capital, we explore how Sufi knowledge, symbols, and literary traditions functioned as vital social assets that facilitated identity formation and integrated the Steppe into the broader Islamic world. By addressing the unique synthesis of pre-Islamic Tengriism with Sufi mysticism, this study illustrates how spiritual practices reshaped collective ethics and social structures, offering a more rigorous understanding of Sufism's role in the historical and cultural evolution of Central Asia.
humanized_abstract: This article investigates the role of Sufism in ancient Turkic nomadic society by focusing on the cultural and anthropological content of Turkic Sufism and its sociological functions. Motives of Sufism in ancient Turkic nomadic culture are reassessed using categories such as collective ritual, charismatic authority and cultural capital. According to Émile Durkheim, rituals result in collective effervescence. In that sense, Turkic Sufi practices like zakir (remembering Allah Muhakames) and semah (a Sufi religious ceremony consisting of a round dance) had transformed the metaphysical values into an emotive lived experience which bonded the nomadic Turkic society. Following Max Weber, the Sufi saints of the era bore “charismatic” qualities, which facilitated the process of the unification of the different tribes under their spiritual leadership and also legitimized the emergence of the new Sufi movement. Additionally, Sufi knowledge, values, and works became a vital cultural capital for the Turkic tribes. It led to the identification of the nomadic society with Islam and at the same time integrated the vast Steppe to the world of Islam. This paper will delve into the integration of Tengriist and Sufi mysticism and explore how spiritual values established a new moral code in the Turkic nomadic society and consequently enhanced the social structure and contributed largely to the historical and cultural development of Central Asia.
